source: a16z Podcast — Is there an AI bubble?
summary: Hyperscaler capex ROI has been strongly positive; debate is whether it continues with Blackwell ramp
summary_extended: Baker argues that the ROI on hyperscaler AI capex has been unambiguously positive to date, citing roughly 10-point increases in ROIC across the major public cloud and AI spenders since they began ramping GPU infrastructure. He contends that the 'AI bubble' debate is largely misframed because the financial returns are already visible in the numbers, not speculative. Baker personally believes ROI will remain positive through the Blackwell buildout cycle, though he acknowledges this forward-looking view is genuinely contested. As a collective buffer against capex risk, he notes these hyperscalers generate approximately $300B in annual free cash flow and hold ~$500B in cash, creating an ~$800B cushion that limits downside even if returns compress. The investment implication is to remain long the major hyperscalers as a group, with the key watch item being whether Blackwell-era spending sustains the ROIC trajectory.
detail: The biggest GPU spenders (all public hyperscalers) have seen ~10-point increases in ROIC since ramping AI capex. Baker personally believes ROI will remain positive through the Blackwell buildout, though he acknowledges it is an open debate. Collectively these companies generate ~$300B free cash flow/year and hold ~$500B cash, providing an ~$800B buffer.
confidence_evidence: Baker says 'there's no debate that thus far the ROI on AI has been really positive' and personally believes it will continue

ticker_audit.reasoning: Alphabet is one of the major public hyperscalers Baker is directly describing — a GPU capex spender that has seen ~10-point ROIC improvement and contributes to the $300B FCF / $500B cash figures cited.
Meta is explicitly among the largest GPU spenders Baker references and is a direct subject of the ROIC improvement thesis, contributing to the collective financial buffer described.
Microsoft is one of the core public hyperscalers Baker is describing, a major AI capex spender whose ROIC improvement is central to the thesis.
Amazon (AWS) is a primary hyperscaler and GPU capex spender directly referenced in the ROIC improvement and FCF/cash buffer thesis.

NVDA is the primary GPU supplier to these hyperscalers and benefits directly from continued Blackwell buildout spending, but the thesis is about hyperscaler ROI, not NVDA itself — making it a direct beneficiary rather than the subject. [RE-AUDIT: NVDA is a GPU supplier to the hyperscalers, not a hyperscaler itself. The thesis is specifically about hyperscaler capex ROI and ROIC improvement. Baker mentions Blackwell as a product cycle context, but the thesis subject is the buyers (hyperscalers), not the seller. NVDA's revenue may benefit from continued capex, but Baker did not specifically connect NVDA's stock or financials to this hyperscaler ROI thesis — it is tangential.]
